content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
May 8, 1997
Michael Shulman
Planning Commissioner
City of Scotts Valley
1 Civic Center Drive
Scoffs Valley, California 95066
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-208
Dear Mr. Shulman:
This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the Political Reform Act (the
“Act”).’
I. QUESTIONS
Do you have a disqualifying conflict of interest, due to your real property holdings, in the
following matters which may come before the planning commission:
(a)
The application for rezoning of the “Glenwood property?”
(b)
The design review decision involving the “Skypark property?”
II. CONCLUSIONS
(a)
Even if the decision results in a material financial effect on your principal
residence due to its proximity to the Glenwood property, you may still participate
because that effect is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
(b)
We are unable to answer this question definitively based on the facts presented.
However, we have given you the relevant legal analysis and set out for you the
factual questions you must investigate in order to answer this question
conclusively.
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No. A-97-208
Page 2
ifi. FACTS
You are a planning commissioner for the City of Scotts Valley, which has a population of
approximately 10,000. There are 3,500 dwelling units in the city, of which 2,400 are single
family dwellings. As a planning commissioner, you may be called upon to participate in
proceedings regarding two matters, the “Glenwood” property and the “Skypark” property.
A.
The Glenwood property.
The Glenwood property is a group ofparcels comprising 224 acres at the northwest
corner ofthe city. An approved specific zoning plan now exists for this property. An application
has been submitted to change the zoning ofthe Glenwood Property.
Your primary residence, a single family dwelling, is located more than 300 feet, but less
than 2,500 feet from the Glenwood property. The lot on which your residence is built is less than
one-quarter acre. Approximately 450 other single family dwellings and 100 multiple family or
mobile/modular dwellings are within 2,500 feet of the Glenwood property. In our April 28,
1997, telephone conversation, you told me that there is no reason to expect that the decision on
the zoning change application will affect your residence in a manner which is substantially
different from the effects on the other dwellings in the area.
2
B.
The Skypark property.
This property is already zoned for commercial development. In our April 28, 1997,
telephone conversation, you characterized the upcoming decision about this property as a “design
review” decision. You expect that a permit of some form, for example, a conditional use permit,
will issue if the application is granted by the planning commission.
You own a condominium which is located more than 300 feet, but less than 2,500 feet
from the property border. The condominium is a rental property. In our April 28, 1997,
telephone conversation, you estimated that there are 300-400 similar rental units within 2,500
feet of the Skypark property. You also said that you expect the impact of the commission
decision regarding the Skypark property to be substantially the same for all the similarly located
rental units.
2 The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. This advice is applicable and
confers immunity (see Section 83114) only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct and that all of the
material facts have been disclosed. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71, 77.)


File No. A-97-208
Page 3
IV. ANALYSIS
A.
Introduction.
The purpose of the Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions is to ensure that public officials
will perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial
interests or the financial interests ofpersons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)
Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. As a public official,
3 you will have a disqualif’ing conflict of
interest with regard to governmental decisions about the Glenwood and Skypark properties ifthe
decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on a financial interest
of yours which is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
B.
Making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision.
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions apply only when a public official makes,
participates in making, or uses or attempts to use his/her official position to influence a
governmental decision. A public official “makes a governmental decision,” when, among other
things, the official, acting within the authority ofhis or her office or position, votes on a matter
or obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action. (Regulation 18700(b).) As a
member of the planning commission, clearly you will be in a position to vote on the matters of
the Glenwood and Skypark properties. Therefore, you may have a conflict ofinterest if the
decisions on these matters materially affect your financial interests.
C.
Financial interests.
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions apply only tofinancial conflicts. A public
official’s financial interest presents a disqualifring conflict of interest if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on the interest.
“Financial interest” is defined, for purposes ofthe Act, in Section 87103. Tn essence, Section
87103 covers six kinds of financial interests (see next paragraph). For purposes of the Act,
“reasonably foreseeable” means a substantial likelihood that a financial effect will occur. (In re
Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether a financial effect is material is determined under
various regulations promulgated by the FPPC, depending upon the nature of the interest and the
degree to which it is involved. (Regulation 18700 et seq.)
Five ofthe six kinds of financial interests are specifically enumerated in subdivisions
(a)-(e) of Section 87103: (a) a business entity in which the public official has an investment of
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to “public officials.” “Public Official,” for purposes
of the Act, is defmed to include every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local agency (with
certain exceptions not relevant here). (Section 82048; Regulation 87100.) As a planning commissioner you are a
public official for purposes of the Act.


File No. A-97-208
Page 4
$1,000 or more; (b) real property in which the public official has an interest of $1,000 or more;
(c) any source of income which aggregates to $290 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision; (d) a business entity in which the public official is an officer, director, manager, etc.;
and, (e) the donor of gifts to the public official if the gifts aggregate to $250 or more within 12
months prior to the decision. Finally, the public official has a financial interest if the
governmental decision will have a “personal effect” on him/her or his/her immediate family,
whether positive or negative, of at least $250 in any 12-month period. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18702.1 (a)(4).)
You have two covered financial interests potentially affected by the planning
commission’s decisions about the Glenwood and Skypark properties; namely, your residence and
the condominium, respectively. Your properties are indirectly involved in the planning
commission’s decisions about the Glenwood and Skypark properties. (See Section 18702.1(b).)
D.
Materiality.
Whether the financial effects of a governmental decision on an indirectly involved real
property interest are material is analyzed under Regulation 18702.3. The two decisions and their
effects on your respective properties are considered separately.
1. The Glenwood property.
For real property interests located more than 300 feet but less than 2,500 feet from the
property which is directly involved in the decision, Regulation 18702.3(a)(3) prescribes two
alternative rules for determining materiality. Ifthe reasonablyforeseeable result of the decision
is that either ofthe following is true, then the effect of the decision is material:
•
the fair market value of the public official’s real property interest will increase or
decrease by $10,000 or more; or
•
the rental value of the property will increase or decrease by $1,000 or more per 12-month
period. (Regulation 1 8702.3(a)(3)(A), (B).)
Thus, ifthe reasonably foreseeable impact ofthe planning commission’s decisions on the
Glenwood property rezoning application is that the fair market value ofyour home increases or
decreases by $10,000 or more, or that the rental value of the home increases or decreases by
$1,000 or more per 12-month period, then the financial effect of that decision on your real
property interest (i.e., your home) is material, and you have a conflict of interest, unless the
“public generally exception” applies. (See part E, below.)
2. The Skypark property.
Your rental condominium is located more than 300 feet but less than 2,500 feet from the
boundaries of the Skypark property. Therefore, you must again apply Regulation 18702.3(a)(3)
to determine whether the impact of that decision on your real property interest, that is, the rental


File No. A-97-208
Page 5
condominium, will be material. Ifthe reasonably foreseeable result of the decision is that the
rental value of the condominium increases or decreases by $1,000 or more per 12-month period,
or if its fair market value increases or decreases by $10,000 or more, then the effect of the
decision is material and you have a conflict of interest, unless the public generally exception
applies.
E.
The public generally exception.
For a disqua4,5’ing conflict of interest to exist, the reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of a governmental decision on the public official’s financial interest must be “distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally.” (Section 87103.) The material financial effect on a
public official’s financial interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally if
the decision will affect a “significant segment” ofthe public “in substantially the same manner”
it affects the public official. (Regulation 18703(a)(l),(2).) “Significant segment” is defined in
Regulation 1 8703(a)(1):
“(1) Significant Segment: The governmental decision will affect a
“significant segment” of the public generally as set forth below:
(A) For decisions that affect the official’s economic interests (excluding
interests in a business entity which are analyzed under subdivision (B)):
(i) Ten percent or more of the population in the jurisdiction of the
official’s agency or the district the official represents, or
(ii) Ten percent or more of all property owners, all home owners, or all
households in the jurisdiction of the official’s agency or the district the official
represents, or
(B) For decisions that affect a business entity in which the official has an
interest as set forth in Section 87103, fifly percent of all businesses in the
jurisdiction or the district the official represents, so long as the segment is
composed ofpersons other than a single industry, trade, or profession; or,
(C) For decisions that affect any ofthe official’s economic interests, the
decision will affect 5,000 individuals who are residents of the jurisdiction; or,
(D) The decision will affect a segment of the population which does not
meet any ofthe standards in subdivisions (a)(1)(A) through (a)(1)(C), however,
due to exceptional circumstances regarding the decision, it is determined such
segment constitutes a significant segment of the public generally.”
“Substantially the same manner” is defined in Regulation 1 8703(a)(2):
“(2) Substantially the Same Manner: The governmental decision will
affect the official’s economic interest in substantially the same manner as it will
affect the significant segment identified in subdivision (a)(1) of this regulation.”


File No. A-97-208
Page 6
1. The Glenwood property.
In addition to the primary “public generally” exception, there are also several “special
purpose” variations ofthe exception.
Ofparticular interest here is the variant focusing on the
principal residence of a public official in a small jurisdiction. Regulation 18703.1, “Public
Generally--Small Jurisdictions; Principal Residence,” provides:
“(a) The effect of a governmental decision on the principal residence of a
public official is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally where
all ofthe following conditions are met:
(1) The public official’s agency has jurisdiction over a population of
25,000 or less.
(2) The decision does not have a direct effect (as provided in Regulation
18702.1(a)(3)) on the real property that serves as the public official’s principal
residence.
(3) The real property that serves as the public official’s principal residence
is more than 300 feet from the boundaries ofthe property which is the subject of
the decision.
(4) There are at least 100 properties under separate ownership which are
within a 2,500 foot radius ofthe boundaries ofthe property which is the subject of
the decision.
(5) The principal residence is located on a parcel of land not more than
one acre in size.
(6) The effect of the decision on the official’s real property interest will be
substantially the same as the effect of the decision on the majority of the
residential properties which are beyond 300 feet, but within 2,500 feet of the
boundaries of the real property that is the subject of the decision.
(b) For purposes of this regulation, “principal residence” means the
domicile of a person, in which the person’s habitation is fixed, wherein the person
has the intention of remaining, and to which the person, whenever he or she is
absent, has the intention of returning. At any given time, a person may have only
one principal residence. With respect to units in condominium complexes,
planned unit developments, and similar residences “the real property that serves as
the public official’s principal residence” and “principal residence,” as used in this
regulation, means the unit or space in which the official has a separate ownership
interest.” (Regulation 18703.1.)
All of the conditions necessary for the exception to apply appear to be satisfied in your
case. First, Scotts Valley has a population of less than 25,000. Second, your residence is not
directly involved in the planning commission’s decision about the Glenwood property rezoning.
Third, your residence is more than 300 feet from the boundary of the Glenwood property.
Fourth, there are more than 100 properties under separate ownership within 2,500 feet of the
boundaries of the Glenwood property. Fifth, your residence is on a lot of less than one acre.
Sixth, you have stated the effect on your property should be indistinguishable from the effect on


File No. A-97-208
Page 7
other residential properties similarly located within 2,500 feet of the Glenwood property’s
boundaries.
(Regulation 18703.l(a)(l)-(6).) Therefore, even ifyou otherwise have a conflict of
interest with regard to the planning commission’s decision on the Glenwood property rezoning
application, the public generally exception applies, and you are not disqualified.
2. The Skypark property.
Assuming that you otherwise have a conflict of interest in the decision about the Skypark
property because of your rental condominium, it must be determined whether the public
generally exception applies. The first step in determining whether the public generally exception
applies is determining whether a “significant segment” of the public is also affected by the
decision. Since a real property interest is involved, a significant segment can be comprised of
any of the following:
•
Ten percent or more ofthe population in the jurisdiction of the planning commission or
the district which you represent; or
•
Ten percent ofmore of all property owners, all home owners, or all households in the
jurisdiction ofthe planning commission or the district which you represent; or
•
Five thousand (5,000) or more individuals who are residents of the jurisdiction; or
•
When exceptional circumstances regarding the decision are such that the segment of the
public affected should be considered significant, even if one ofthe above thresholds are
not met. (Regulation 1 8703(a)(l)(A)-(D).)
When identifying the relevant segment of the public, it must be borne in mind that this
segment must be affected in substantially the same manner as the public official in question. In
your case, this segment, however comprised, must be affected in substantially the same manner
as are you as an owner ofa rental condominium unit. It is not enough that, for example, ten
percent of all property owners are somehow affected; they must be affected as are you as an
owner of a rental condominium unit. This determination, obviously, depends highly on the
particular facts of each situation. It is much more of afactual than a legal analysis.
Based on the facts available to us, we are not in a position to make this determination.
4
You must acquire the facts necessary to determine, for example, whether ten percent of all
property owners or 5,000 or more individuals are affected by the Skypark decisions in
substantially the same manner as are you as an owner of a rental condominium.
You have given us information about the number of “units” of housing. The public generally exception
is stated in terim of, among other things, “property owners,” “homeowners,” and “households.” (See Regulation
18703(a)(1)(A).) We are not in position to make the assumptions necessary to equate “units” with, say,
“households.”


File No. A-97-208
Page 8
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General C unsel
By: J John Verg11i
/ Staff Couisel, Legal Division
SGC:JV:ak

conclusively.

Regulation 1 8703(a)(1):

“significant segment” of the public generally as set forth below:
(A) For decisions that affect the official’s economic interests (excluding
interests in a business entity which are analyzed under subdivision (B)):
(i) Ten percent or more of the population in the jurisdiction of the
official’s agency or the district the official represents, or
(ii) Ten percent or more of all property owners, all home owners, or all
households in the jurisdiction of the official’s agency or the district the official
represents, or
(B) For decisions that affect a business entity in which the official has an
interest as set forth in Section 87103, fifly percent of all businesses in the
jurisdiction or the district the official represents, so long as the segment is
composed ofpersons other than a single industry, trade, or profession; or,
(C) For decisions that affect any ofthe official’s economic interests, the
decision will affect 5,000 individuals who are residents of the jurisdiction; or,

meet any ofthe standards in subdivisions (a)(1)(A) through (a)(1)(C), however,
due to exceptional circumstances regarding the decision, it is determined such
segment constitutes a significant segment of the public generally.”
“Substantially the same manner” is defined in Regulation 1 8703(a)(2):

affect the official’s economic interest in substantially the same manner as it will
affect the significant segment identified in subdivision (a)(1) of this regulation.”
1. The Glenwood property.
In addition to the primary “public generally” exception, there are also several “special
purpose” variations ofthe exception.
Ofparticular interest here is the variant focusing on the
principal residence of a public official in a small jurisdiction. Regulation 18703.1, “Public
Generally--Small Jurisdictions; Principal Residence,” provides:
“(a) The effect of a governmental decision on the principal residence of a
public official is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally where
all ofthe following conditions are met:
(1) The public official’s agency has jurisdiction over a population of
25,000 or less.
(2) The decision does not have a direct effect (as provided in Regulation
18702.1(a)(3)) on the real property that serves as the public official’s principal
residence.
(3) The real property that serves as the public official’s principal residence
is more than 300 feet from the boundaries ofthe property which is the subject of
the decision.
(4) There are at least 100 properties under separate ownership which are
within a 2,500 foot radius ofthe boundaries ofthe property which is the subject of
the decision.
(5) The principal residence is located on a parcel of land not more than
one acre in size.
(6) The effect of the decision on the official’s real property interest will be
substantially the same as the effect of the decision on the majority of the
residential properties which are beyond 300 feet, but within 2,500 feet of the
boundaries of the real property that is the subject of the decision.
(b) For purposes of this regulation, “principal residence” means the
domicile of a person, in which the person’s habitation is fixed, wherein the person
has the intention of remaining, and to which the person, whenever he or she is
absent, has the intention of returning. At any given time, a person may have only
one principal residence. With respect to units in condominium complexes,
planned unit developments, and similar residences “the real property that serves as
the public official’s principal residence” and “principal residence,” as used in this
regulation, means the unit or space in which the official has a separate ownership
interest.” (Regulation 18703.1.)
All of the conditions necessary for the exception to apply appear to be satisfied in your
case. First, Scotts Valley has a population of less than 25,000. Second, your residence is not
directly involved in the planning commission’s decision about the Glenwood property rezoning.
Third, your residence is more than 300 feet from the boundary of the Glenwood property.
Fourth, there are more than 100 properties under separate ownership within 2,500 feet of the
boundaries of the Glenwood property. Fifth, your residence is on a lot of less than one acre.
Sixth, you have stated the effect on your property should be indistinguishable from the effect on
other residential properties similarly located within 2,500 feet of the Glenwood property’s
boundaries.
(Regulation 18703.l(a)(l)-(6).) Therefore, even ifyou otherwise have a conflict of
interest with regard to the planning commission’s decision on the Glenwood property rezoning
application, the public generally exception applies, and you are not disqualified.
2. The Skypark property.
Assuming that you otherwise have a conflict of interest in the decision about the Skypark
property because of your rental condominium, it must be determined whether the public
generally exception applies. The first step in determining whether the public generally exception
applies is determining whether a “significant segment” of the public is also affected by the
decision. Since a real property interest is involved, a significant segment can be comprised of
any of the following:
•
Ten percent or more ofthe population in the jurisdiction of the planning commission or
the district which you represent; or
•
Ten percent ofmore of all property owners, all home owners, or all households in the
jurisdiction ofthe planning commission or the district which you represent; or
•
Five thousand (5,000) or more individuals who are residents of the jurisdiction; or
•
When exceptional circumstances regarding the decision are such that the segment of the
public affected should be considered significant, even if one ofthe above thresholds are
not met. (Regulation 1 8703(a)(l)(A)-(D).)
When identifying the relevant segment of the public, it must be borne in mind that this
segment must be affected in substantially the same manner as the public official in question. In
your case, this segment, however comprised, must be affected in substantially the same manner
as are you as an owner ofa rental condominium unit. It is not enough that, for example, ten
percent of all property owners are somehow affected; they must be affected as are you as an
owner of a rental condominium unit. This determination, obviously, depends highly on the
particular facts of each situation. It is much more of afactual than a legal analysis.
Based on the facts available to us, we are not in a position to make this determination.
4
You must acquire the facts necessary to determine, for example, whether ten percent of all
property owners or 5,000 or more individuals are affected by the Skypark decisions in
substantially the same manner as are you as an owner of a rental condominium.
You have given us information about the number of “units” of housing. The public generally exception
is stated in terim of, among other things, “property owners,” “homeowners,” and “households.” (See Regulation
18703(a)(1)(A).) We are not in position to make the assumptions necessary to equate “units” with, say,
“households.”

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Do you have a disqualifying conflict of interest, due to your real property holdings, in the
following matters which may come before the planning commission:
(a)
The application for rezoning of the “Glenwood property?”
(b)
The design review decision involving the “Skypark property?”

CONCLUSION: (a)
Even if the decision results in a material financial effect on your principal
residence due to its proximity to the Glenwood property, you may still participate
because that effect is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
(b)
We are unable to answer this question definitively based on the facts presented.
However, we have given you the relevant legal analysis and set out for you the
factual questions you must investigate in order to answer this question
conclusively.
(916) 322-5660
ifi. FACTS
You are a planning commissioner for the City of Scotts Valley, which has a population of
approximately 10,000. There are 3,500 dwelling units in the city, of which 2,400 are single
family dwellings. As a planning commissioner, you may be called upon to participate in
proceedings regarding two matters, the “Glenwood” property and the “Skypark” property.
A.
The Glenwood property.
The Glenwood property is a group ofparcels comprising 224 acres at the northwest
corner ofthe city. An approved specific zoning plan now exists for this property. An application
has been submitted to change the zoning ofthe Glenwood Property.
Your primary residence, a single family dwelling, is located more than 300 feet, but less
than 2,500 feet from the Glenwood property. The lot on which your residence is built is less than
one-quarter acre. Approximately 450 other single family dwellings and 100 multiple family or
mobile/modular dwellings are within 2,500 feet of the Glenwood property. In our April 28,
1997, telephone conversation, you told me that there is no reason to expect that the decision on
the zoning change application will affect your residence in a manner which is substantially
different from the effects on the other dwellings in the area.
2
B.
The Skypark property.
This property is already zoned for commercial development. In our April 28, 1997,
telephone conversation, you characterized the upcoming decision about this property as a “design
review” decision. You expect that a permit of some form, for example, a conditional use permit,
will issue if the application is granted by the planning commission.
You own a condominium which is located more than 300 feet, but less than 2,500 feet
from the property border. The condominium is a rental property. In our April 28, 1997,
telephone conversation, you estimated that there are 300-400 similar rental units within 2,500
feet of the Skypark property. You also said that you expect the impact of the commission
decision regarding the Skypark property to be substantially the same for all the similarly located
rental units.
2 The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. This advice is applicable and
confers immunity (see Section 83114) only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct and that all of the
material facts have been disclosed. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71, 77.)

CONCLUSIONS (a) Even if the decision results in a material financial effect on your principal residence due to its proximity to the Glenwood property, you may still participate because that effect is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
